sourceQuestion:
Let L_R be a regular language and L_2 be a context-free language. Which of the following languages is/are context-free?
A. L_R / L_2 (right quotient of L_R by L_2)
B. complement(L_R) union L_2
C. L_R intersection L_2
D. L_R - L_2 (set difference)

parsed.stem: Let L_R be a regular language and L_2 be a context-free language. Which of the following languages is/are context-free?

options.text: L_R / L_2 (right quotient of L_R by L_2)

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?
A. Every subset of a recursively enumerable language is recursive.
B. If a language L and its complement are both recursively enumerable, then L must be recursive.
C. Complement of a context-free language must be recursive.
D. If L_1 and L_2 are regular, then L_1 ∩ L_2 must be deterministic context-free.

parsed.stem: Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

options.text: Every subset of a recursively enumerable language is recursive.
If a language L and its complement are both recursively enumerable, then L must be recursive.
Complement of a context-free language must be recursive.
If L_1 and L_2 are regular, then L_1 ∩ L_2 must be deterministic context-free.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following languages:
L_1 = {ww^R | w in {a, b}^+}
L_2 = {a^n b^n | n >= 1}
Note that w^R is the reversal of the string w. Which of the following is/are TRUE?
A. L_1 and L_2 are regular.
B. L_1 and L_2 are context-free.
C. L_1 is regular and L_2 is context-free.
D. L_1 and L_2 are context-free but not regular.

options.text: L_1 is regular and L_2 is context-free.
L_1 and L_2 are context-free but not regular.

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is/are CORRECT?
A. The intersection of two regular languages is regular.
B. The intersection of two context-free languages is context-free.
C. The intersection of two recursive languages is recursive.
D. The intersection of two recursively enumerable languages is recursively enumerable.

parsed.stem: Which of the following statements is/are CORRECT?

options.text: The intersection of two regular languages is regular.
The intersection of two context-free languages is context-free.
The intersection of two recursive languages is recursive.
The intersection of two recursively enumerable languages is recursively enumerable.

sourceQuestion:
Let L be a regular language and M be a context-free language, both over the alphabet Sigma. Let L' and M' denote the complements of L and M respectively. Which of the following statements about the language L' union M is TRUE?
A. It is necessarily regular but not necessarily context-free.
B. It is necessarily context-free.
C. It is necessarily non-regular.
D. None of the above.

parsed.stem: Let L be a regular language and M be a context-free language, both over the alphabet Sigma. Let L' and M' denote the complements of L and M respectively. Which of the following statements about the language L' union M is TRUE?

options.text: It is necessarily regular but not necessarily context-free.

sourceQuestion:
The language {(0^P)^P | P is a prime} is:
A. regular
B. context-free but not regular
C. context-free but its complement is not context-free
D. not context-free

parsed.stem: The language {(0^P)^P | P is a prime} is:

options.text: context-free but its complement is not context-free

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following languages:
L_1 = {a^i b^j | i != j, i >= 0, j >= 0}
L_2 = {a^i b^j | i = 2j, i >= 0}
Which of the following is true?
A. L_1 is not a CFL but L_2 is.
B. L_1 and L_2 are both CFLs and L_2 is non-regular.
C. L_1 is not a CFL but L_2 is.
D. There is a 4-state PDA that accepts L_1, but there is no DPDA that accepts L_2.
sourceAnswer: B. L_1 and L_2 are both CFLs and L_2 is non-regular.

options.text: L_1 is not a CFL but L_2 is.
L_1 and L_2 are both CFLs and L_2 is non-regular.
L_1 is not a CFL but L_2 is.
There is a 4-state PDA that accepts L_1, but there is no DPDA that accepts L_2.

sourceQuestion:
Let L be the set of all binary strings whose last two symbols are the same. The number of states in the minimal state deterministic finite state automaton accepting L is:
A. 2
B. 5
C. 8
D. 3

A. 3 states
B. 5 states
C. n+2 states
D. None of the above

sourceQuestion:
Given an arbitrary non-deterministic finite automaton (NFA) with N states, the maximum number of states in an equivalent minimized DFA is at least:
A. N^2
B. 2^N
C. 2N
D. N!

sourceQuestion:
Consider a DFA over {a, b} accepting all strings which have number of a's divisible by 6 and number of b's divisible by 8. What is the minimum number of states that the DFA will have?
A. 14
B. 48
C. 15
D. 24

A. 2 states
B. 3 states
C. 4 states
D. 5 states

Consider the regular language L = (111 + 11111)*. The minimum number of states in any DFA accepting this language is:
A. 3
B. 5
C. 8
D. 9

sourceQuestion:
A minimum state deterministic finite automaton accepting the language
L = { w in {0,1}* | number of 0s and 1s in w are divisible by 3 and 5, respectively }
has:
A. 15 states
B. 11 states
C. 10 states
D. 9 states

Consider the following Finite State Automaton (FSA) with states {q0, q1, q2, q3, q4}, alphabet {0,1}, start state q0, and accept states {q2, q4}. The transitions are: q0 --0--> q1, q0 --1--> q3, q1 --0--> q2, q1 --1--> q2, q2 --0--> q2, q2 --1--> q2, q3 --0--> q4, q3 --1--> q4, q4 --0--> q4, q4 --1--> q4. The minimum state automaton equivalent to the above FSA has the following number of states:
A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

sourceQuestion:
Let w be any string of length n in {0,1}*. Let L be the set of all substrings of w. What is the minimum number of states in a non-deterministic finite automaton that accepts L?
A. n - 1
B. n
C. n + 1
D. 2^(n-1)

parsed.stem: Let w be any string of length n in {0,1}*. Let L be the set of all substrings of w. What is the minimum number of states in a non-deterministic finite automaton that accepts L?

sourceQuestion:
Definition of a language L with alphabet {a} is given as following:
L = { a^k | k > 0, and n is a positive integer constant }
What is the minimum number of states needed in a DFA to recognize L?
A. k + 1
B. n + 1
C. 2^(n-1)
D. 2^n

Let L be the language represented by the regular expression Sigma*00(1)Sigma* where Sigma = {0, 1}. What is the minimum number of states in a DFA that recognizes L_bar (complement of L)?

A. 4
B. 5
C. 6
D. 8

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

sourceQuestion:
Let N be an NFA with n states. Let k be the number of states of a minimal DFA which is equivalent to N. Which one of the following is NECESSARILY true?
A. k >= 2^n
B. k <= 2^n
C. k <= n^2
D. k >= n

parsed.stem: Let N be an NFA with n states. Let k be the number of states of a minimal DFA which is equivalent to N. Which one of the following is NECESSARILY true?

sourceQuestion:
Let N be an NFA with n states and let M be the minimized DFA with m states recognizing the same language. Which of the following is NECESSARILY true?
A. m <= 2^n
B. m <= n^2
C. M has one accept state
D. m <= n

parsed.stem: Let N be an NFA with n states and let M be the minimized DFA with m states recognizing the same language. Which of the following is NECESSARILY true?

sourceQuestion:
In which of the cases stated below is the following statement true?
"For every non-deterministic machine M_1 there exists an equivalent deterministic machine M_2 recognizing the same language."
A. M_1 is non-deterministic finite automaton.
B. M_1 is non-deterministic PDA.
C. M_1 is a non-deterministic Turing machine.
D. For no machines M_1 and M_2, the above statement is true.

parsed.stem:
In which of the cases stated below is the following statement true?
"For every non-deterministic machine M_1 there exists an equivalent deterministic machine M_2 recognizing the same language."

options.text: M_1 is a non-deterministic Turing machine.
For no machines M_1 and M_2, the above statement is true.

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following conversions is not possible (algorithmically)?
A. Regular grammar to context free grammar
B. Non-deterministic FSA to deterministic FSA
C. Non-deterministic PDA to deterministic PDA
D. Non-deterministic Turing machine to deterministic Turing machine
sourceAnswer: C. Non-deterministic PDA to deterministic PDA

parsed.stem: Which of the following conversions is not possible (algorithmically)?

options.text: Regular grammar to context free grammar
Non-deterministic Turing machine to deterministic Turing machine

sourceQuestion:
Regarding the power of recognition of languages, which of the following statements is false?
A. The non-deterministic finite-state automata are equivalent to deterministic finite-state automata.
B. Non-deterministic Push-down automata are equivalent to deterministic Push-down automata.
C. Non-deterministic Turing machines are equivalent to deterministic Turing machines.
D. Multi-tape Turing machines are equivalent to Single-tape Turing machines.
sourceAnswer: B. Non-deterministic Push-down automata are equivalent to deterministic Push-down automata.

parsed.stem: Regarding the power of recognition of languages, which of the following statements is false?

options.text: The non-deterministic finite-state automata are equivalent to deterministic finite-state automata.
Non-deterministic Push-down automata are equivalent to deterministic Push-down automata.
Non-deterministic Turing machines are equivalent to deterministic Turing machines.
Multi-tape Turing machines are equivalent to Single-tape Turing machines.

sourceQuestion:
Let N_f and N_p denote the classes of languages accepted by non-deterministic finite automata and non-deterministic push-down automata, respectively. Let D_f and D_p denote the classes of languages accepted by deterministic finite automata and deterministic push-down automata respectively. Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. D_f subset N_f and D_p subset N_p
B. D_f = N_f and D_p = N_p
C. D_f = N_f and D_p subset N_p
D. D_f subset N_f and D_p = N_p
sourceAnswer: C. D_f = N_f and D_p subset N_p

parsed.stem: Let N_f and N_p denote the classes of languages accepted by non-deterministic finite automata and non-deterministic push-down automata, respectively. Let D_f and D_p denote the classes of languages accepted by deterministic finite automata and deterministic push-down automata respectively. Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.text: D_f subset N_f and D_p subset N_p
D_f = N_f and D_p subset N_p
D_f subset N_f and D_p = N_p

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following pairs have DIFFERENT expressive power?
A. Deterministic finite automata (DFA) and Non-deterministic finite automata (NFA)
B. Deterministic push down automata (DPDA) and Non-deterministic push down automata (NPDA)
C. Deterministic single tape Turing machine and Non-deterministic single tape Turing machine
D. Single tape Turing machine and Multi-tape Turing machine
sourceAnswer: B. Deterministic push down automata (DPDA) and Non-deterministic push down automata (NPDA)

parsed.stem: Which of the following pairs have DIFFERENT expressive power?

options.text: Deterministic finite automata (DFA) and Non-deterministic finite automata (NFA)
Deterministic push down automata (DPDA) and Non-deterministic push down automata (NPDA)
Deterministic single tape Turing machine and Non-deterministic single tape Turing machine
Single tape Turing machine and Multi-tape Turing machine

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following statements is FALSE?
A. There exist context-free languages such that all the context-free grammars generating them are ambiguous.
B. An unambiguous context-free grammar always has a unique parse tree for each string of the language generated by it.
C. Both deterministic and non-deterministic pushdown automata always accept the same set of languages.
D. A finite set of strings over some alphabet is always a regular language.
sourceAnswer: C. Both deterministic and non-deterministic pushdown automata always accept the same set of languages.

parsed.stem: Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

options.text: There exist context-free languages such that all the context-free grammars generating them are ambiguous.
An unambiguous context-free grammar always has a unique parse tree for each string of the language generated by it.
Both deterministic and non-deterministic pushdown automata always accept the same set of languages.
A finite set of strings over some alphabet is always a regular language.

sourceQuestion:

Which one of the following can be a pumping length (the constant guaranteed by the pumping lemma) for L?
A. 3
B. 5
C. 9
D. 24

sourceQuestion:
A language L satisfies the Pumping Lemma for regular languages, and also the Pumping Lemma for context-free languages. Which of the following statements about L is TRUE?
A. L is necessarily a regular language.
B. L is necessarily a context-free language, but not necessarily a regular language.
C. L is necessarily a context-sensitive language, but not necessarily a context-free language.
D. None of the above.

parsed.stem: A language L satisfies the Pumping Lemma for regular languages, and also the Pumping Lemma for context-free languages. Which of the following statements about L is TRUE?

options.text: L is necessarily a regular language.
L is necessarily a context-free language, but not necessarily a regular language.
L is necessarily a context-sensitive language, but not necessarily a context-free language.

sourceAnswer: A. delta(q_0, a, a) = {(q_1, epsilon)}, delta(q_0, b, b) = {(q_1, epsilon)}

options.text: delta(q_0, a, a) = {(q_1, epsilon)}, delta(q_0, b, b) = {(q_1, epsilon)}
delta(q_0, a, a) = {(q_1, a)}, delta(q_0, b, b) = {(q_1, b)}
delta(q_0, a, a) = {(q_1, epsilon)}, delta(q_0, b, b) = {(q_1, epsilon)}, delta(q_0, epsilon, 1) = {(q_1, epsilon)}
delta(q_0, a, a) = {(q_1, aa)}, delta(q_0, b, b) = {(q_1, bb)}
